q3: The main recurring issue was Section 2 stopping at diagnosis without completing the argumentative arc — it took several rounds of feedback to get the section to land on a prescription rather than just naming the problem. That's a structural instinct the model should have caught earlier.
q5: I looked at it, and it does capture the things I care about reasonably well — the human-first framing, the claim-before-evidence structure, the graduated recommendations. My one reservation is that the rubric describes these as discrete criteria with priority rankings, which makes them feel more mechanical than they actually are in practice. Good policy writing is about how these elements reinforce each other, not about checking them in order. The model still needed my feedback to get Section 2 right, which suggests the rubric alone wasn't sufficient.
q6: It made me notice that the model is actually quite good at the opening — audience-first framing clicked fairly quickly — but consistently weaker at completing argumentative arcs within individual sections. The rubric's 'Claim-Before-Evidence' criterion names that pattern, but seeing it labeled made me realize the model tends to do the diagnosis half well and underinvest in the prescription half. That's a useful thing to know.

q3: The structural problem in the middle section — getting from evidence to the specific policy prescription, not just the general conclusion — took multiple rounds to resolve. The model understood the diagnosis each time I named it, but didn't anticipate it proactively. It also kept needing reminders that the opening can't lead cold on statutory framework; that preference had to be stated more than once.
q5: I looked at it, and it maps reasonably well onto what I care about — 'Claim-Before-Evidence Structure' captures the principle/example/recommendation pattern I rely on, and 'Strategic Pragmatism in Recommendations' gets the graduated ask right. But the rubric frames these as structural rules, and for me they're more about reader experience — the question I'm always asking is 'does a skeptical commissioner stay with this?' That's a slightly different orientation than the rubric's checklist framing suggests.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice that the model was consistently strong on the criteria it could execute locally — a well-glossed term, a clean section header — but weaker on the criteria that require holding the whole document in view, like whether the opening's human stakes persist into the technical sections. The rubric names that as a priority but the model treated it more like a one-time task at the top of the document rather than a thread to maintain throughout.

q3: The model kept treating citation and authority as the same thing — it would drop a statutory reference and assume that was enough, without explaining what the statute actually is or why it carries weight. I had to push on the FDCPA point multiple times before it finally earned its place in the argument rather than just floating there as a credential.
q5: I looked at it, and it captures a lot of what I care about — the claim-before-evidence structure and the graduated recommendations criteria in particular map closely to how I actually think about these documents. What it doesn't fully capture is the tone calibration piece: the difference between urgency and alarmism is subtle and the rubric gestures at it without giving the model enough to work with. The model was still slightly prone to overstatement early in the session.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model is actually quite good at structural compliance once it has a framework — it hit the skimmability criteria cleanly once I asked for headers, and the recommendations got specific fast. What it surfaced is that the model's weakness is inferential bridging: it can state a principle and give an example but it often needs explicit prompting to connect them to the specific policy ask, which is exactly the gap between 'academic' and 'useful' in this kind of writing.
